q: Predict the output: integer overflow in unchecked context.

answer.oneLiner: Prints -2147483648 which is int.MinValue. C# arithmetic is unchecked by default, so adding 1 to int.MaxValue wraps silently to the most negative int value.
answer.detail: An int holds 32 bits. int.MaxValue is 2147483647 (binary: 0111...1111). Adding 1 flips the sign bit, producing 1000...0000 in two's complement, which is -2147483648 (int.MinValue). Because C# defaults to unchecked arithmetic for performance, no exception is thrown. The CPU simply wraps. To detect this, wrap the code in a checked {} block or use the /checked compiler flag. Inside a checked block, the same operation throws OverflowException.
keyPoints: C# arithmetic is unchecked by default - overflow wraps silently.
int.MaxValue + 1 wraps to int.MinValue due to two's complement arithmetic.
A checked {} block throws OverflowException on overflow instead of wrapping.
Use checked when correctness matters more than raw performance.

code.note: Use checked {} or long for quantities that can exceed int range in production systems.

answer.analogy: Unchecked overflow is like an odometer rolling from 999999 back to 000000 with no warning.
redFlags: Saying an OverflowException is thrown by default.
Not knowing that unchecked is the C# default and why.
Saying the result is undefined behavior like in C or C++.
Not knowing that checked {} blocks or the checked keyword can make overflow detectable.

followUpsQA.q: How do you make the overflow throw an exception instead?
followUpsQA.a: Wrap the operation in a checked {} block or use the checked() expression form. The compiler emits overflow-checking instructions and throws OverflowException at runtime.

followUpsQA.q: Why is unchecked the default in C#?
followUpsQA.a: Performance. Overflow checking requires extra CPU instructions on every arithmetic operation. Most code never overflows, so the default favors speed. Use checked explicitly where correctness is critical.

followUpsQA.q: Does this affect floating-point types like double?
followUpsQA.a: No. Floating-point types (float, double) follow IEEE 754 and produce positive or negative infinity on overflow, not wrapping. OverflowException and checked only apply to integer arithmetic.

followUpsQA.q: What type would you use for financial totals that could exceed int range?
followUpsQA.a: Use long for large integer counts or decimal for monetary values. Decimal has 28-29 significant digits and avoids the binary floating-point rounding issues that double has for currency.

dialogue.text: I write int a = int.MaxValue and then int b = a + 1. What does Console.WriteLine(b) print?

-2147483648, which is int.MinValue. C# defaults to unchecked arithmetic. Adding 1 to the maximum positive 32-bit value flips the sign bit in two's complement, wrapping silently to the most negative int value. No exception is thrown.
How do you make it throw instead of wrapping?

Wrap the expression in a checked block: int b = checked(a + 1). That tells the compiler to emit overflow-detecting instructions. At runtime, the overflow triggers OverflowException. You can also enable checked arithmetic project-wide with the /checked compiler switch, but that is rarely done because it impacts performance.
Why would unchecked overflow be a security vulnerability?

Integer overflow can bypass security checks. For example, if you compute a buffer size as count * elementSize and it overflows to a small positive number, you allocate a tiny buffer but then write count * elementSize bytes into it - a classic buffer overrun. CWE-190 (Integer Overflow) is on OWASP's list specifically for this reason.
What alternative type avoids this issue entirely for large counters?

Use long for integer counts - it goes up to 9.2 * 10^18. For money use decimal which has 28-29 significant digits and no binary rounding. .NET 7 also introduced System.Numerics.BigInteger for arbitrarily large integers if you need that.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

q: Predict the output: multicast delegate return value.

answer.oneLiner: Prints 8. All three handlers run in subscription order, but for a multicast Func the runtime only keeps the return value of the last invoked handler. The earlier return values are discarded.
answer.detail: A delegate in C# can hold multiple method references - this is called a multicast delegate. When you invoke it, the runtime walks through the invocation list in order, calling each method. For void delegates (Action) this is the entire story. For delegates with a return value (Func), each handler runs but only the last one's return value is passed back to the caller. The intermediate return values are computed and then thrown away. d(5) calls x+1=6, then x+2=7, then x+3=8. Only 8 comes back.
keyPoints: All handlers in a multicast delegate run in subscription order.
For Func delegates, only the last handler's return value is returned to the caller.
Earlier return values are computed but discarded.
To collect all return values, use GetInvocationList() and invoke each delegate individually.

code.note: Multicast Func returns only the last handler's value. Use GetInvocationList() to collect all results.

answer.analogy: Multicast Func is like asking three people a question in a row - you only remember the last answer.
redFlags: Saying the result is 6 because only the first handler runs.
Saying the results are aggregated or summed automatically.
Not knowing that all handlers actually execute even though only one result is returned.
Not knowing about GetInvocationList as the solution.

followUpsQA.q: Do all three handlers actually execute?
followUpsQA.a: Yes. All three run in subscription order. The distinction is that only the last return value is propagated to the caller. The other two execute their bodies but their returned values are silently discarded.

followUpsQA.q: How do you collect the return value of every handler?
followUpsQA.a: Call GetInvocationList() which returns the individual delegates. Cast each to the delegate type and invoke it. Collect the results in a list or process them as needed.

followUpsQA.q: What happens if one handler throws an exception?
followUpsQA.a: Execution stops at the throwing handler. Subsequent handlers in the list are not called. The exception propagates to the caller. Use GetInvocationList with try/catch per invocation to run all handlers regardless of exceptions.

followUpsQA.q: Why does C# have this last-value-wins behavior instead of throwing a compile error for Func?
followUpsQA.a: Historical design - multicast was designed primarily for events which use Action (no return value). Func works as a multicast delegate because all delegates share the same multicast infrastructure, but the last-wins behavior is a consequence rather than a deliberate design for Func.

dialogue.text: I combine three lambdas into a multicast Func<int,int> and call it with 5. What is the return value?

8, which is 5 + 3 from the last lambda. All three handlers run: the first returns 6, the second returns 7, the third returns 8. The runtime discards 6 and 7 and returns only the last handler's result to the caller.
If I needed all three results, how would I get them?

Call d.GetInvocationList() to get each delegate in the chain separately. Cast each to Func<int,int> and invoke with 5. Collect into an array - you get [6, 7, 8]. This is the only way to access intermediate return values.
When would a multicast Func be dangerous in production?

In a pricing engine where multiple discount functions are chained as a multicast Func<decimal, decimal>. A developer adds a new discount handler expecting it to apply on top of the others. Instead, only the last handler's result is returned and all previous discounts are silently ignored. The bug shows up only in specific scenarios.
What is the right pattern for composing multiple transformations?

Chain them functionally rather than via multicast: var result = handler1(handler2(handler3(price))). Or use a pipeline pattern with a List<Func<decimal,decimal>> and fold over it: var result = handlers.Aggregate(price, (acc, fn) => fn(acc)). This makes the composition explicit and predictable.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

q: Predict the output: explicit double-to-int cast.

answer.oneLiner: Prints 10. An explicit (int) cast truncates toward zero - it drops the fractional part entirely. It does not round to the nearest integer.
answer.detail: Casting a double to int discards everything after the decimal point by moving toward zero. So 10.9 becomes 10, 10.1 also becomes 10, and -10.9 becomes -10 (not -11). This is truncation, not rounding. Math.Round(10.9) would give 11 using banker's rounding by default. Math.Ceiling(10.9) gives 11 (always rounds up). Math.Floor(10.9) gives 10 (always rounds down toward negative infinity). The difference between Floor and truncation matters for negative numbers: (int)(-0.5) is 0 (toward zero) but Math.Floor(-0.5) is -1.
keyPoints: Explicit (int) cast truncates toward zero - not toward negative infinity.
(int)10.9 is 10 and (int)-10.9 is -10, both truncated toward zero.
Math.Round gives nearest integer (11 for 10.9).
Math.Floor rounds toward negative infinity; Math.Ceiling rounds toward positive infinity.

code.note: Cast truncates toward zero. Use Math.Round, Math.Floor, or Math.Ceiling for controlled rounding.

answer.analogy: Casting to int is a paper guillotine - it cuts off everything after the decimal point without looking at how close you were to the next number.
redFlags: Saying (int)10.9 returns 11 because it rounds.
Confusing truncation with rounding.
Not knowing the difference between Math.Floor (toward negative infinity) and truncation (toward zero).
Not knowing that Math.Round uses banker's rounding by default in C#.

followUpsQA.q: What does (int)(-10.9) return?
followUpsQA.a: -10. Truncation always goes toward zero. -10.9 truncated toward zero is -10, not -11. Math.Floor(-10.9) would return -11.

followUpsQA.q: What rounding mode does Math.Round use by default?
followUpsQA.a: Banker's rounding (MidpointRounding.ToEven). Math.Round(2.5) returns 2 and Math.Round(3.5) returns 4 - it rounds to the nearest even number at the midpoint. Use MidpointRounding.AwayFromZero for traditional rounding.

followUpsQA.q: Can a double-to-int cast throw an exception?
followUpsQA.a: Not in unchecked mode (default). If the double value is outside int range, the result is undefined (implementation-specific). In a checked block, it throws OverflowException for out-of-range values.

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between Math.Floor and Math.Truncate?
followUpsQA.a: Math.Floor always rounds toward negative infinity. Math.Truncate always rounds toward zero. They differ for negative numbers: Math.Floor(-1.5) is -2 but Math.Truncate(-1.5) is -1.

dialogue.text: I write double d = 10.9 and int i = (int)d. What does i hold?
10. The explicit (int) cast truncates toward zero - it drops the .9 entirely without rounding. 10.9 becomes 10, not 11.
What about (int)(-10.9)?
-10. Truncation toward zero means we remove the fractional part in the direction of zero. For a negative number, -10.9 truncated is -10, not -11. If you wanted -11 you would use Math.Floor(-10.9).
What is banker's rounding and when does it matter?

Math.Round by default uses MidpointRounding.ToEven - it rounds 0.5 to the nearest even integer. So Math.Round(2.5) is 2 and Math.Round(3.5) is 4. This reduces statistical bias over large datasets. For financial calculations where traditional rounding is expected, pass MidpointRounding.AwayFromZero explicitly.
If you were computing a shipping cost rounded to the nearest cent, how would you do it safely?

Use decimal instead of double to avoid binary floating-point drift, then Math.Round(cost, 2, MidpointRounding.AwayFromZero) to round to 2 decimal places with predictable midpoint behavior. Never cast to int for money - you lose the cents entirely.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

answer.oneLiner: Prints 99. The ?? operator evaluates left to right and returns the first non-null value. x is null, y is null, so the fallback 99 is used.
answer.detail: The ?? (null-coalescing) operator is right-associative but evaluates logically left to right when chained: x ?? y ?? 99 is parsed as x ?? (y ?? 99). It checks x first. x is null, so it checks y. y is null, so it evaluates the final fallback 99. Because 99 is non-null, it is assigned to z. The assignment to int z does not throw because the final value is a non-null int. If y had been 5, z would be 5 and 99 would never be evaluated.
keyPoints: ?? returns the left operand if it is not null, otherwise the right operand.
A chain evaluates left to right until a non-null value is found.
The assignment to int z is valid because the final non-null value is an int.
0 is not null - ?? only triggers on null, not on falsy or zero values.

code.note: ?? only substitutes for null - not for 0, false, or empty string.

answer.analogy: ?? is a backup generator - it only switches on if the main power (left side) is completely absent, not just dim.
redFlags: Saying ?? throws because both x and y are null.
Thinking ?? also triggers on 0, false, or empty string.
Not knowing the right-associative parsing of chained ?? operators.
Confusing ?? with the C conditional ternary for null checks.

followUpsQA.q: What does z equal if y is 5 instead of null?
followUpsQA.a: z equals 5. The chain stops at the first non-null value. x is null so it moves to y; y is 5 (non-null) so 5 is returned and the 99 fallback is never reached.

followUpsQA.q: What is the ??= operator?
followUpsQA.a: The null-coalescing assignment operator. x ??= defaultValue assigns defaultValue to x only if x is currently null. It is shorthand for: if (x == null) x = defaultValue.

followUpsQA.q: Does ?? work with value types that are not nullable?
followUpsQA.a: No. A plain int cannot be null, so ?? has no meaning. You must use int? (Nullable<int>) for the left operand to be possibly null. The fallback (right side) can be a plain int.

followUpsQA.q: How does ?? differ from the ternary operator for null checks?
followUpsQA.a: x ?? y is exactly equivalent to x != null ? x : y but evaluates x only once. The ternary forces you to write x twice and risks evaluating x twice if it has side effects.

dialogue.text: int? x = null, int? y = null, int z = x ?? y ?? 99. What is printed?
99. The ?? chain evaluates left to right. x is null so it moves right. y is null so it moves right again. The fallback 99 is non-null so it is returned and assigned to z.
Would the assignment to int z throw if both x and y were null and the fallback were also null?

If the fallback were int? and had value null, yes - assigning a null Nullable<int> to a plain int throws InvalidOperationException at runtime. But in this case the fallback is the literal 99 which cannot be null, so the compiler is happy.
What is a real scenario where a three-level ?? chain is useful?

Configuration layering. In an e-commerce app, a product's shipping weight might come from user input first, then from the product category default, then from a global fallback: decimal weight = userOverride ?? categoryDefault ?? globalDefault. This expresses priority cleanly in one readable line.
What is ??= and when would you use it?

It is the null-coalescing assignment: connection ??= new SqlConnection(connectionString). If connection is already set it is left alone. If it is null it gets initialized. It is the idiomatic lazy initialization pattern in C# when you do not need thread safety.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

q: Predict the output: try/catch/finally with a handled exception.

answer.oneLiner: Prints B then C. The divide-by-zero throws before WriteLine A can run. The catch block prints B. The finally block always runs and prints C.
answer.detail: When the division by zero throws, control jumps immediately to the matching catch block. The WriteLine(A) line inside the try never executes. The catch prints B and handles the exception. After the catch (or after the try if no exception occurred), the finally block always runs and prints C. Finally runs on every code path: normal completion, handled exception, unhandled exception (before the stack unwinds further), and even if a return statement is inside the try.
keyPoints: Finally always runs regardless of whether an exception was thrown or caught.
A statement after the throw point inside try is never reached.
Finally runs after the catch block, not before.
Finally does not run only if the process is forcibly terminated (Environment.FailFast, power loss).

code.note: Use finally (or using) to guarantee resource cleanup regardless of exceptions.

answer.analogy: Finally is a seatbelt - it engages whether the drive was smooth or ended in a crash.
redFlags: Saying A prints because the try block starts before the exception.
Saying finally only runs when there is no exception.
Not knowing the order: catch runs first, then finally.
Saying finally does not run for an unhandled exception.

followUpsQA.q: Does finally run if the exception is NOT caught?
followUpsQA.a: Yes. If no catch handles the exception, finally still runs before the exception continues unwinding the stack to the next outer try block.

followUpsQA.q: Does finally run if a return statement is inside the try block?
followUpsQA.a: Yes. The return value is computed, stored, and then finally runs before the method actually returns to the caller.

followUpsQA.q: What is one scenario where finally does not run?
followUpsQA.a: Environment.FailFast() bypasses finally blocks entirely. Also, a StackOverflowException or a corrupted state exception can kill the process before finally runs.

followUpsQA.q: How does the using statement relate to finally?
followUpsQA.a: using is syntactic sugar for try/finally. using (var r = new Resource()) { } compiles to try { } finally { r.Dispose(); }. It guarantees Dispose is called on IDisposable objects.

dialogue.text: The try block divides by zero which throws. There is a catch for DivideByZeroException that prints B, and a finally that prints C. What is the complete output?

B then C. The division throws before WriteLine(A) executes, so A is never printed. Control jumps to the catch which prints B. After the catch completes, the finally block runs unconditionally and prints C.
What if there were a return statement inside the catch before C printed in finally?

C would still print. Finally always executes even when the catch contains a return. The runtime stores the return value, executes the finally block, and then returns from the method. Finally cannot be skipped by return.
What is the practical use of finally in database code?

Closing connections and releasing transactions. You open a connection in the try, do work, and in finally you close the connection. If an exception occurs mid-query the finally guarantees the connection is returned to the pool. The using statement is the cleaner way to express this - it compiles to try/finally under the hood.
Can you throw from inside a finally block?

You can, but it is dangerous. If an exception is already being propagated and the finally throws a new one, the original exception is lost - replaced by the new one. This makes debugging much harder. Finally blocks should only do cleanup, never throw.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

q: Predict the output: nested try - inner finally vs outer catch order.

answer.oneLiner: Prints 'Inner Finally' then 'Outer Catch'. When an exception propagates out of the inner try, the inner finally runs during stack unwinding before the outer catch handles the exception.
answer.detail: When a DivideByZeroException is thrown inside the inner try, the runtime looks for a matching catch. The inner try has no catch, so the exception starts unwinding. Before leaving the inner scope, the inner finally block executes - printing 'Inner Finally'. Then the exception continues propagating to the outer try, where the outer catch handles it - printing 'Outer Catch'. The rule is: finally blocks for the scope being exited run before handlers in outer scopes are considered.
keyPoints: Finally runs as the exception unwinds past its scope, before outer catch handlers fire.
Order: inner finally, then outer catch.
If the inner had its own catch, it would handle the exception locally and the outer catch would not fire.
Each layer of try/finally is cleaned up in order from innermost to outermost.

code.note: Inner finally executes during unwinding before the outer catch handler is entered.

answer.analogy: Think of nested try/finally as peeling an onion - you peel (finally) each layer before reaching the core (outer catch).
redFlags: Saying 'Outer Catch' prints before 'Inner Finally'.
Not knowing that finally runs during exception unwinding, not after it.
Assuming an uncaught inner exception skips its own finally.
Confusing the order when the exception is re-thrown inside the catch.

followUpsQA.q: What prints if the inner try catches the exception itself?
followUpsQA.a: Only 'Inner Finally' followed by nothing from the outer catch. If the inner catch handles the exception, the outer catch never sees it.

followUpsQA.q: What if both the inner and outer have a finally?
followUpsQA.a: Both run. Inner finally runs first during unwinding, then the outer catch fires, and after the outer catch (or outer try if no exception) the outer finally runs.

followUpsQA.q: Does re-throwing the exception inside the outer catch affect whether finally ran?
followUpsQA.a: No. The inner finally already ran when the exception first propagated past its scope. Re-throwing inside the outer catch starts a new propagation cycle outward.

followUpsQA.q: In what order does finally execute in a deep call stack?
followUpsQA.a: From innermost to outermost. Each scope's finally runs as the exception unwinds past it. The order mirrors the stack frame order in reverse.

dialogue.text: An inner try throws a DivideByZeroException. The inner try has a finally but no catch. The outer try has a catch. What is the print order?

'Inner Finally' then 'Outer Catch'. The exception is thrown, the runtime starts unwinding. Before leaving the inner try's scope, the inner finally runs and prints 'Inner Finally'. Then the exception reaches the outer catch which prints 'Outer Catch'.
What would change if the inner try also had a catch that printed 'Inner Catch'?

The inner catch would handle the exception, printing 'Inner Catch'. Then the inner finally would still run and print 'Inner Finally'. The outer catch would not fire at all because the exception was already handled.
In a real transaction management scenario, why does this ordering matter?

You want to rollback the transaction before logging the error. The inner finally ensures rollback happens as the exception exits the database scope. The outer catch then logs the error after the transaction is already clean. If the order were reversed, you might log an error for a transaction still open, which could mislead diagnostics.
What happens to the inner finally if the outer catch re-throws the exception?

Nothing changes for the inner finally - it already ran. Re-throwing sends the exception further up the call stack. Any outer finally blocks above the current one would then run in their turn as the exception continues unwinding.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

q: Predict the output: async ordering with await.

answer.oneLiner: Prints 1, 2, 4 then (after the delay) 3. Code runs synchronously up to the first real suspension point. When Task.Delay suspends Work, control returns to the caller which prints 4. Then 3 resumes after the delay.
answer.detail: async methods run synchronously until they hit an await on an incomplete task. Run() prints 1 and calls Work(). Work() prints 2 and awaits Task.Delay(100) - this is the first real suspension. At this point Work gives up the thread and control returns to Run()'s await on Work(). Run() then gives up the thread and control returns to the code that called Run(), which is the synchronous code after the var t = Run() call. That code prints 4. Then t.Wait() blocks until the task completes. After the 100ms delay, the continuation in Work resumes and prints 3.
keyPoints: Async code runs synchronously up to the first incomplete await.
await yields control back to the caller when the awaited task is not yet done.
Code after an await resumes as a continuation when the task completes.
t.Wait() blocks the calling thread until the task finishes.

code.note: Without await, control returns to the caller at the first suspension. Await if you need sequential behavior.

answer.analogy: async/await is like starting a slow oven - you press start (1,2), walk away to do something else (4), then come back when the timer rings (3).
redFlags: Saying the output is 1, 2, 3, 4 because async runs sequentially.
Not knowing that await on an incomplete task yields to the caller.
Confusing CPU-bound and IO-bound async - the key is whether the task is already complete.
Not knowing what t.Wait() does vs await t.

followUpsQA.q: When would 3 print before 4?
followUpsQA.a: If the awaited task completed synchronously (e.g., Task.Delay(0) or a cached completed task). When the awaited task is already done, the continuation runs inline without yielding. But this is an optimization detail, not guaranteed behavior.

followUpsQA.q: What is the difference between t.Wait() and await t?
followUpsQA.a: t.Wait() blocks the current thread synchronously until the task finishes. await t suspends the current async method and returns control to the caller without blocking a thread. Wait() can cause deadlocks in UI or ASP.NET contexts.

followUpsQA.q: What is the state machine that the compiler generates?
followUpsQA.a: The compiler transforms async methods into a class implementing IAsyncStateMachine. Each await point becomes a state transition. The state machine resumes at the correct state when the awaited task completes.

followUpsQA.q: What is ConfigureAwait(false) and when do you use it?
followUpsQA.a: It tells the awaiter not to capture and resume on the original synchronization context. Use it in library code to avoid deadlocks in UI applications and to improve performance by not marshaling back to the original thread.

dialogue.text: Run() prints 1, calls Work() which prints 2 and awaits Task.Delay. Run() then has more code printing 3. After calling Run() there is a Console.WriteLine(4). What is the order?

1, 2, 4, then 3. Run() starts synchronously and prints 1. It calls Work() which prints 2 and hits await Task.Delay - an incomplete task. Work suspends and returns an incomplete Task to Run(). Run() reaches its await on that incomplete Task and suspends too, returning control to the caller. The caller prints 4. After 100ms the delay completes, Work resumes and prints 3.
What would happen if I removed the delay and used await Task.CompletedTask instead?

The order would be 1, 2, 3, 4. Task.CompletedTask is already complete, so await Task.CompletedTask does not actually suspend. The continuation runs inline. Run() continues synchronously through Work, prints 3, finishes, and only then does the caller print 4.
In an ASP.NET Core controller, why is t.Wait() dangerous?

ASP.NET Core does not have a synchronization context, so Wait() is less likely to deadlock there than in classic ASP.NET or WPF. But it still blocks a thread pool thread for the duration of the wait, which harms throughput. The right approach is to await all the way up - async Action methods are fully supported in controllers.
How does the compiler know where to resume after an await?

It generates a state machine class. Each await becomes a numbered state. When the task completes, it calls MoveNext on the state machine with the correct state number. The method body is encoded as a switch over state numbers, and execution jumps to the right continuation point.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

q: Predict the output: static field initializer vs static constructor order.

answer.oneLiner: Prints 'Field' then 'Constructor'. Static field initializers run in textual order before the static constructor body executes.
answer.detail: When a type is first accessed, the CLR runs type initialization exactly once. The order is: first, all static field initializers run in the order they appear in the source code. Second, the static constructor body runs. This ordering is guaranteed by the C# specification. The field initializer static int x = Print('Field') calls Print which writes 'Field'. Then the static constructor body runs and writes 'Constructor'. Type initialization is thread-safe and lazy - it runs on first use, and only once.
keyPoints: Static field initializers run before the static constructor body.
Field initializers run in textual (source code) order.
Type initialization is triggered on first use of the type.
The CLR guarantees type initialization is thread-safe and runs exactly once.

code.note: Static field initializers run in textual order. Declare fields in dependency order.

answer.analogy: Static initialization is like setting up a shop: arrange the shelves (field initializers) before you open the doors (static constructor).
redFlags: Saying the static constructor runs before the field initializers.
Assuming static initialization is not thread-safe.
Not knowing when type initialization is triggered.
Saying static initialization runs multiple times.

followUpsQA.q: What triggers static type initialization?
followUpsQA.a: First access to any static member of the type - a static field read or write, a static method call, or creating an instance of the type. The CLR ensures it happens at most once.

followUpsQA.q: Is static initialization thread-safe?
followUpsQA.a: Yes. The CLR uses a lock to ensure the static constructor runs exactly once even in a multi-threaded environment. Subsequent accesses see the completed initialized state without locking.

followUpsQA.q: What is the Singleton pattern relationship to static initialization?
followUpsQA.a: The classic lazy singleton uses a static readonly field: static readonly Singleton instance = new Singleton(). Type initialization guarantees it runs once and is thread-safe, making this pattern safe without explicit locking.

followUpsQA.q: What happens if an exception is thrown inside a static constructor?
followUpsQA.a: The type is permanently marked as failed. Any future attempt to access the type throws TypeInitializationException wrapping the original exception. The static constructor never runs again.

dialogue.text: A class has a static field initializer that calls a Print method and a static constructor body. Accessing the class triggers initialization. What prints and in what order?

'Field' then 'Constructor'. The C# spec guarantees that static field initializers run first, in textual order, before the static constructor body. So Print('Field') runs from the field initializer, then the static constructor body runs and writes 'Constructor'.
Does the order change if the field is declared after the static constructor in the source file?

No. Static field initializers always run before the static constructor body regardless of textual order relative to the constructor. But field initializers run among themselves in textual order. So if you have field A before field B, A's initializer runs before B's.
What real bug can this ordering cause?

A field initializer that references another static field of the same class that is declared later in the source. The later field is still at its default value (null or 0) when the earlier initializer runs. This is a silent bug - no compile error, just an unexpected null or zero at runtime.
What happens if the static constructor throws?

The type is permanently broken. The runtime marks the type initialization as failed. Every subsequent access to any member of that type throws TypeInitializationException wrapping the original exception. The static constructor will never be retried, so you must restart the application to recover.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

q: Predict the output: passing null to a params array.

answer.oneLiner: Print() with no arguments prints 0. Print(null) throws NullReferenceException. No arguments creates an empty array; explicit null passes null as the array reference itself.
answer.detail: The params keyword lets callers pass zero or more arguments. When called with no arguments, the compiler synthesizes an empty array and passes that. So n.Length is 0. When called with the literal null, the caller explicitly passes null as the int[] parameter - no array is created. Inside the method, n is null and calling n.Length throws NullReferenceException. This is a subtle distinction: no arguments creates an empty array; null is a null reference.
keyPoints: No arguments for params creates an empty array - n.Length is 0, no exception.
Passing null explicitly passes null as the array reference - n.Length throws NullReferenceException.
Guard against null: check n != null or use n?.Length ?? 0 inside the method.
You can pass a real array as the params argument: Print(new int[]{1,2,3}).

code.note: Always null-check params arrays. No arguments and null are two different things.

answer.analogy: No args is like a delivery driver arriving with an empty van. Null is the driver calling to say there is no van at all.
redFlags: Saying both Print() and Print(null) behave identically.
Saying Print() throws because no array is provided.
Not knowing that the compiler synthesizes an empty array for no-argument params calls.
Not knowing how to guard against null in a params method.

followUpsQA.q: How do you null-guard a params parameter?
followUpsQA.a: Check for null at the top of the method: if (n == null) return; or use n?.Length ?? 0 for the length. Some developers use n ?? Array.Empty<int>() to normalize null to empty.

followUpsQA.q: Can you pass an existing array to a params parameter?
followUpsQA.a: Yes. Print(new int[]{1,2,3}) passes the array directly. The compiler does not wrap it in another array. This is equivalent to calling Print(1, 2, 3).

followUpsQA.q: What happens if you declare both a params overload and a single-argument overload?
followUpsQA.a: The compiler prefers the most specific match. A single int argument matches the specific overload. Multiple ints or no argument match the params overload. If the resolution is ambiguous the compiler reports an error.

followUpsQA.q: Is there a performance cost to params?
followUpsQA.a: Yes. Every call site with arguments allocates a new array on the heap. In performance-sensitive loops, prefer overloads that take explicit parameters. C# 12 introduced params Span<T> which can use stack allocation.

dialogue.text: A method takes params int[] n and prints n.Length. I call it with no arguments and then with null. What happens each time?

No arguments prints 0. The compiler synthesizes an empty array and passes it, so n is non-null and n.Length is 0. Passing null explicitly sends null as the array reference. n.Length then throws NullReferenceException because you are accessing a property on a null reference.
How would you harden this method?

Null-check at the entry: if (n == null) n = Array.Empty<int>(); This normalizes null to empty so the rest of the method sees a safe, non-null array. Alternatively check and return early if null represents an invalid call rather than 'no items'.
Is there a performance downside to params?

Every call with arguments allocates a new int[] on the heap. In a tight loop that calls a params method thousands of times, that is thousands of small allocations causing GC pressure. The fix in hot paths is to provide explicit overloads: Print(int a), Print(int a, int b) and have the params version as a fallback for the general case.
What did C# 12 add related to params?

params Span<T> and params ReadOnlySpan<T>. If the arguments are known at the call site, the compiler can stack-allocate the array instead of heap-allocating it. This eliminates the allocation entirely for small argument lists and makes params practical in performance-sensitive code.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

q: Predict the output: covariant assignment of IEnumerable<T>.

answer.oneLiner: Prints 1. IEnumerable<T> is covariant (out T), so IEnumerable<Dog> is directly assignable to IEnumerable<Animal>. No copy is made - it is the same sequence.
answer.detail: Generic covariance means a more-derived type argument can be used where a less-derived one is expected, but only when the type parameter appears only in output positions (returned from methods, not accepted as parameters). IEnumerable<out T> is covariant because T only appears as a return type (Current property). You can only enumerate Animals out of it - you cannot put a Cat into a sequence typed as IEnumerable<Dog>. So the assignment IEnumerable<Animal> animals = dogs is safe and no copy is needed. Count() returns 1 because there is one Dog. List<Dog> cannot be assigned to List<Animal> because List<T> is invariant - its methods both produce and consume T.
keyPoints: IEnumerable<out T> is covariant - IEnumerable<Dog> is assignable to IEnumerable<Animal>.
Covariance only works when T appears only in output positions.
No copy is made - both variables reference the same underlying sequence.
List<T> is invariant - List<Dog> cannot be assigned to List<Animal>.

code.note: Covariance works on interfaces with 'out T'. List<T> is invariant and does not support this.

answer.analogy: Covariance is like a read-only shelf of Dogs - you can hand it to anyone who wants to browse Animals because they can only look, never add a Cat.
redFlags: Saying List<Dog> is assignable to List<Animal>.
Not knowing what covariance means or what 'out T' indicates.
Thinking the assignment copies the collection.
Confusing covariance (out) with contravariance (in).

followUpsQA.q: Why can't List<Dog> be assigned to List<Animal>?
followUpsQA.a: List<T> is invariant. If you could assign a List<Dog> to List<Animal>, you could then call animals.Add(new Cat()) which would corrupt the Dog list at runtime. The compiler prevents this.

followUpsQA.q: What does the 'out' in IEnumerable<out T> mean?
followUpsQA.a: It means T only appears in output positions (returned from members). This makes the interface covariant. You can use a more-derived T wherever a less-derived T is expected, because you can only get values out, never put a wrong type in.

followUpsQA.q: What is contravariance and which interfaces use it?
followUpsQA.a: Contravariance (in T) means a less-derived type argument can be used where a more-derived one is expected. IComparer<Animal> can be used where IComparer<Dog> is needed. Action<Animal> is assignable to Action<Dog>.

followUpsQA.q: How do you convert a List<Dog> to an IEnumerable<Animal>?
followUpsQA.a: Cast or assign directly: IEnumerable<Animal> animals = dogList. The covariance of IEnumerable allows this. You can also use dogs.Cast<Animal>() but that is redundant here since covariance handles it automatically.

dialogue.text: I assign an IEnumerable<Dog> to IEnumerable<Animal>. Does it compile? What does Count() return?

Yes, it compiles. IEnumerable<T> is declared with 'out T' which means it is covariant. IEnumerable<Dog> is a subtype of IEnumerable<Animal> because Dog is a subtype of Animal and T only appears in output positions. Count() returns 1 because the underlying list has one Dog.
Would the same assignment work with List<Dog> to List<Animal>?

No, compile error CS0029. List<T> is invariant because it has methods that both consume and produce T: Add(T item) takes a T in and indexer returns T out. If the assignment were allowed, you could call animals.Add(new Cat()) on what is actually a List<Dog>, which would corrupt it at runtime. The compiler blocks this.
What is an example of contravariance in .NET?

IComparer<in T>. If you have a method that compares Animals, it can compare Dogs too because Dog is an Animal. So an IComparer<Animal> is assignable to IComparer<Dog>. Action<in T> is the other common example - an action that accepts an Animal can accept a Dog, so Action<Animal> is assignable to Action<Dog>.
How does covariance affect LINQ query results?

It lets you write repository methods that return IEnumerable<Dog> and pass those results to code expecting IEnumerable<Animal> without casting. This is the main practical benefit - you can code against base types in consumer code without wrapper methods or explicit casts.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅
